data.title: U.S. stocks are mixed after weaker-than-expected payrolls, lowering odds of a rate hike
data.description:
Wall Street’s major indexes were mixed on Thursday due to a weaker-than-expected June non-farm payrolls report that lowered expectations for the Federal Reserve to hike interest rates and a tech trade that continues to struggle.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -0.3%, while the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was +0.7%, adding to new highs. The heavy-tech Nasdaq (COMP:IND) was last -1.1% as some tech giants such as Tesla (TSLA) and Sandisk (SNDK) declined.

The U.S. economy added 57,000 jobs in June, falling short of the 115,000 increase economists surveyed by Dow Jones had expected. The unemployment rate, however, unexpectedly declined to 4.2%, versus forecasts for it to hold at 4.3%.

Moreover, initial jobless claims fell to 215,000 for the week ended June 27, beating estimates.

Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh said on Wednesday that the Fed will “chart a new course” on forward guidance, contrasting with his predecessor's practice of indicating which way policymakers were leaning in upcoming meetings.

Odds of an interest rate hike before the end of the year fell, while odds for a 25-basis point cut rose, according to the CME FedWatch Tool.

Bond yields were unchanged. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was flat at 4.49%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) was also unchanged at 4.18%.

In addition, the U.S. and Iran are conducting negotiations in Doha that focus on the Strait of Hormuz. The Trump administration is trying to convince Iran that it will gain more from a nuclear deal than from tolls in the strait.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) were slightly higher at $68, while Brent (CO1:COM) was $71 per barrel.

data.title: Silicom falls 10% despite analyst upgrade after AI-driven rally
data.description:
• Shares of Silicom (SILC) fell about 10% on Thursday, extending a volatile week even after Needham upgraded the network infrastructure company to Buy from Hold and set a $60 price target following its first production order tied to AI inference.
  • The stock had surged 14% on Tuesday after Silicom announced the order, followed by a 7.8% decline on Wednesday.
  • Brokerage says AI inference revenue could reach several million dollars in 2026 and grow to tens of millions of dollars in 2027, earlier than previously expected.
  • Needham said the new order and additional customer engagements could support faster revenue growth and improve the company's path to profitability.

data.title: Western Alliance stock falls after Jefferies sues over First Brands debacle
data.description:
Western Alliance Bancorporation (WAL) stock dipped 3.1% in Thursday afternoon trading after Jefferies Financial Group (JEF) sued the company for freezing a $25M account after the collapse of auto parts company First Brands.

Jefferies (JEF) stock climbed 3.2%.

A lawsuit filed in New York state court on Wednesday alleged that Western Alliance (WAL) froze a deposit account held by Point Bonita Capital, a unit of Jefferies' Acadia Asset Management that provided receivables funding to First Brands, Bloomberg News reported.

The suit from Jefferies comes after Western Alliance (WAL) sued Jefferies in March, alleging that Point Bonita failed to repay a loan backed by First Brands receivables and deceived it into a refinancing arrangement. Since then, executives at First Brands have been charged with falsifying or inflating the amount of receivables.

Point Bonita had invested $715M in receivables owed to First Brands when the company filed for bankruptcy. Its chance of recovering much of that amount is dim. Part of the fund's exposure was pledged into a leveraged facility with Western Alliance (WAL), which is expected to result in a $126M charge.

From Jefferies' account of the matter, Western Alliance said its deposit agreement with Point Bonita lets it freeze the account while the litigation proceeds. "That is demonstrably false," Jefferies contended, accusing Western Alliance of "grabbing assets to which it had no right" ahead of any court decision.

In March, Jefferies' CEO and president told shareholders and clients that "Jefferies has no obligation to pay off a non-recourse loan Western Alliance chose to make to a special purpose vehicle against First Brands receivables."

On Thursday, a spokesperson for Western Alliance (WAL) told Bloomberg, "We are confident in the merits of our case, will continue to take appropriate steps to protect the interests of the company and our stakeholders, and look forward to presenting the facts through the legal process."

data.title: Novartis gene therapy Itvisma approved in EU for spinal muscular atrophy
data.description:
Novartis (NVS) on Thursday announced that the European Commission approved its gene therapy. Itvisma (onasemnogene abeparvovec) for children aged two years and older with spinal muscular atrophy, a rare neuromuscular disorder.

Accordingly, the one-time therapy will be available across all 27 EU member states as well as Norway, Iceland, and Liechtenstein for 5q SMA attributed to a mutation in the survival motor neuron 1 (SMN1) gene.

Itvisma, designed to replace the SMN1 gene, is the first and only gene replacement therapy approved to treat a broad group of SMA patients in the EU, the Swiss drugmaker said.

The company already markets another SMA therapy, branded as Zolgensma, as the only gene replacement therapy available for SMA patients younger than two years.

“Together with Zolgensma, we can now offer gene replacement therapy options across different stages of SMA in Europe, from newborns to adults,” Patrick Horber, President of Novartis’ international operations, said.

The decision came after an expert panel of the EU drug regulator recommended the approval in April based on results from the company’s pivotal STEER study, in which Itvisma led to a statistically significant improvement on a scale used to measure SMA disease progression.

data.title: Greenbrier downgraded at Susquehanna on low railcar orders
data.description:
Greenbrier (GBX) down 3.3% in Thursday's trading as Susquehanna downgrades the railroad equipment supplier to Neutral from Positive with a $52 price target, citing the company's declining order backlog.

Greenbrier (GBX) is "positioned for higher highs and lower lows (manufacturing efficiencies; leasing shift), but without visibility on both improved builds and tariff clarity, we're hopping on the sidelines," Susquehanna's Harrison Bauer says, downgrading shares "until we can get constructive on applying early-cycle valuation or visibility into capitalizing FY28."

The company's backlog is at its lowest level since 2010 at ~13.8K units after receiving its lowest order total in nearly 10 years, which management attributed to broad-based capital investmenthesitancy, but Bauer says the backdrop is driving lower visibility into 2027, adding that recovery may not materialize until H2 2027 even if orders start to improve.

The analyst says he expects a stronger recovery later in 2027 or into 2028 rather than a near-term inflection, adding that "rising backlogs historically support elevated early-cycle multiples, but until we have confidence or evidence in backlog/orders improving, we're not willing to underwrite a higher multiple."

data.title: Microsoft merging enterprise, consumer Copilot AI chatbots: report
data.description:
• Microsoft (MSFT) is merging its enterprise and consumer Copilot artificial intelligence chatbots as the tech giant looks to make it more ubiquitous, The Information reported.
  • The move to merge the two apps into a single app comes after Microsoft executive Jacob Andreou sent a memo on the matter, the news outlet added, citing the memo itself.
  • In addition to simplifying its offerings, Andreou told Microsoft employees the new Copilot app will also include AI coding tools and new AI agents. The AI coding tools and AI agents would have additional paid features, the news outlet added.
  • The Redmond, Wa.-based Microsoft is looking to ship the new version of Copilot by August, the news outlet added, citing someone with knowledge of the effort.
  • The company did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.
  • Microsoft shares were higher in midday trading on Thursday.

data.title: AI memory, chip stocks slump as sell-off continues amid global peers tumbling
data.description:
Shares of memory and AI-related stocks tumbled even further on Thursday after opening in the green following yesterday's sell-off.

Wall Street’s major indexes were mixed on Thursday due to a weaker-than-expected June non-farm payrolls report that lowered expectations for the Federal Reserve to hike interest rates, and chip stocks dragging tech down. The tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) fell about 1.35%. The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) declined about 0.55%, but the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was the silver lining and rose about 0.42%. According to the latest survey conducted by the American Association of Individual Investors, or AAII, investor sentiment deteriorated sharply, with bearish views surging and optimism fading.

Wednesday's tech sell-off had pressured Asian peers, which opened in the red and tumbled during the day. South Korea’s Kospi Index slumped about 7.89%. South Korean memory chip giant SK Hynix (HXSCL) plummeted nearly 15%, while peer Samsung Electronics (SSNLF) tumbled around 9% on the Korea Exchange. Earlier this week, the two countries pledged billions in investment in South Korea aimed at growing the country's semiconductors and AI space. Today the two provided details. 

SK Hynix said it would invest 100T won (about $64.38B) to build memory chip ‌and packaging plants as part of a South Korean investment drive, which was announced earlier this week, aimed at semiconductors and AI. Meanwhile, Samsung plans to invest a combined 140T won ($90B) in South Korea's central Chungcheong region, expanding production of display panels, batteries, semiconductors, and chip materials. Reportedly, SK Hynix is removing price caps from its Long-Term Agreements, or LTAs, recently signed with its clients. Analysts suggest that suppliers' negotiating power is being significantly strengthened as the company, along with rival Samsung, extends contract periods.

"Samsung and SK Hynix now make up around half the Kospi’s total weight, up from around just a quarter at the end of last year," Zavier Wong, analyst at eToro, said in a note, according to CNBC. This highlights the companies' huge influence on the country’s benchmark index.

Hong Kong and Chinese tech stocks also saw red. China’s largest contract chipmaker and foundry Semiconductor Manufacturing International tumbled about 10% in Hong Kong, while Hua Hong Grace, another Chinese chip foundry listed in Hong Kong, slumped nearly 13%.

American memory giant Micron Technology (MU) — which competes with Samsung and SK Hynix to provide High-Bandwidth Memory, or HBM chips to Nvidia — fell nearly 5% on Thursday. Sandisk (SNDK) plummeted nearly 13%, while Western Digital (WDC) slumped about 10%. During the second quarter of 2026, Night Watch Investment Management LP had trimmed its position in Western Digital. Seagate Technology (STX) also slumped about 10%.

Shares of AI chipmaker Nvidia (NVDA) dipped around 2%, while Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) slumped nearly 5%. Qualcomm (QCOM) also fell about 5%, while Broadcom (AVGO) declined around 3%. Cerebras (CBRS) slumped about 7%.

Other AI and networking-related stocks also tumbled on Thursday. Applied Optoelectronics (AAOI) plummeted about 17%, while Corning (GLW) slumped nearly 11%. Coherent (COHR) tumbled around 10%, while Lumentum (LITE) fell nearly 9%. Ciena (CIEN) and Celestica (CLS) each declined about 8%. Arista Networks (ANET) fell nearly 5%, while Cisco (CSCO) declined about 4%.

Shares of Marvell Technology (MRVL), GlobalFoundries (GFS), and Lattice Semiconductor (LSCC) each tumbled nearly 10%. Arm (ARM) slumped about 7%, while Intel (INTC) fell nearly 5% despite positive news. HSBC raised Intel's price target to a street-high of $200 from $100, while keeping its Buy rating. Meanwhile, Wedbush noted that Tesla's hiring of Gary Jiang, an 18-year veteran of Intel, to help with its Terafab project is considered a potential “positive” for the chip giant.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) and Analog Devices (ADI) each fell about 3%, while Texas Instruments (TXN) dipped nearly 2%.

Aehr Test Systems (AEHR) slumped about 20%. Aehr was removed from a broad range of Russell indexes — including the Russell 3000E and Russell Microcap as well as the Russell 2000 Value —during the annual index reconstitution, which went into effect on June 27.

Chip equipment makers: KLA (KLAC) slumped about 13%, Lam Research (LRCX) tumbled nearly 11%, and Applied Materials (AMAT) each declined about 10%. ASML (ASML) fell nearly 5% on Thursday.

Update: The story was updated with stock price movement.

data.title: SA analyst upgrades/downgrades: RKLB, XOM, QCOM, DELL
data.description:
Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Rocket Lab (RKLB) have received upgrades based on favorable industry dynamics and strategic acquisitions, respectively. Meanwhile, Dell Technologies (DELL) and Qualcomm (QCOM) face downgrades as analysts point to stretched valuations and elevated execution risks following significant rallies.

Upgrades

  • Exxon Mobil (XOM): Upgrade to Buy by Envision Research. The analyst cites multi-decade lows in both U.S. and global oil inventories, along with a depleted Strategic Petroleum Reserve, as catalysts for a near-term price rebound combined with recent valuation compression.

    "With both US and global inventories at such low levels, I see large odds for a restocking in the near term (say in the next 1–2 years). As an example, according to the OECD model quoted above, the refiling of the global inventory is projected to begin in early 2027. I further expect the refilling of both US and global reserves to act as a catalyst for oil price recovery."

  • Rocket Lab (RKLB): Upgrade to Speculative Buy by JR Research. The analyst views the Iridium acquisition as a pivotal move that provides critical L-band spectrum access and proven EBITDA profitability, positioning the company to compete more effectively against integrated players.

    "For RKLB, I think we need to see management providing more hard numbers in terms of clarity on how this Iridium M&A can really reshape its growth trajectory. That's kind of an ambivalent moment right now, which could either lift its buying sentiments or see the selling pressure intensify."

Downgrades

  • Dell Technologies (DELL): Downgrade to Hold by The Alpha Analyst. After delivering approximately 360% returns, the analyst believes the stock now trades at fair-to-expensive valuations with diminished asymmetry, recommending profit taking.

    "The asymmetry that lower valuations and a prospective earnings revision spree ahead provided even in January this year is no longer available. The demand strength and overall fundamentals do show sufficient strength to keep the stock supported—but the upside route from here is not more obvious than downside risks today."

  • Qualcomm (QCOM): Downgrade to Hold by Kumquat Research. The analyst notes that Qualcomm's pivot from smartphones to AI chips introduces heightened volatility, margin pressure, and significant execution risk over a lengthy transition period.

    "Qualcomm is now leaving the comfort of a domain that it has dominated for decades and is entering uncharted territory; with that move comes execution risk and margin risks, for which current shareholders might not have the risk tolerance."

data.title: Cumberland Farms files for IPO
data.description:
Massachusetts-based convenience store chain Cumberland Farms filed a registration statement with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission ahead of a potential initial public offering.

The number of shares and price range have yet to be determined.

The shares will trade on the Nasdaq under the ticker symbol CMBY.

The company began as a one-cow dairy farm in Cumberland, Rhode Island, in 1939, with the first dairy store opening in Bellingham, Massachusetts, 20 years later. The chain eventually spread across New England and Florida, largely by acquiring Gulf and Chevron gas stations in 10 Northeastern states in the mid-1980s and then 200 Exxon stations in 2003.

With 1,500 retail locations, Cumberland Farms is one of the fastest-growing convenience store chains in the U.S.   

In addition to Cumberland Farms, the parent company’s portfolio includes Certified Oil, Fastrac, Kwik Shop, Loaf’N Jug, Minit Mart, Sprint Food Stores, Tom Thumb, Turkey Hill, and Quik Stop stores.

The IPO relates solely to Cumberland Farms.

data.title: Boeing making progress in reducing Air Force One technical challenges, GAO says
data.description:
Boeing (BA) has reduced some of the technical challenges that have delayed its delivery of two jets that will serve as Air Force One, the Government Accountability Office said in its annual assessment of U.S. weapons programs, Bloomberg reported Thursday.

The company likely will deliver the first of the modified jets by 2028 and has made progress on "completing the engineering design for the environmental control system, resolving cabin decompression issues, and hiring and retaining more qualified mechanics," the GAO said.

The Air Force One modifications, which started in 2020, still need "detailed designs for the aircraft interiors, fabrication of the wire bundles, and rework to correct defects in structural modifications," and repairs of stress corrosion cracks on the two planes "are ongoing and are expected to be completed" this year, officials told GAO.

Boeing (BA) remains nearly three years behind its original schedule to deliver the jets, GAO said, with each estimated to cost $3.3B, or ~7% more than last year's projection.

Separately, UPS (UPS) said it relied on Boeing's (BA) assessment that a known engine mount issue did not pose a flight safety risk when it chose not to adopt enhanced inspections before last year's fatal cargo plane crash in Louisville, according to newly released National Transportation Safety Board filings.

In its post-hearing submission to the NTSB, UPS (UPS) said it followed all required Boeing (BA) and Federal Aviation Administration-approved maintenance programs for its MD-11 fleet.
